source_metadata.title_raw: Bill Peake  - A-18-023 - March 5, 2018 - Pacific Grove

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 5, 2018

Bill Peake
Pacific Grove City Councilmember
300 Forest Avenue
Pacific Grove, CA 93950

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-023

Dear Mr. Peake:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of
fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide
assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these
decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you, a Pacific Grove City Councilmember, from taking part in
governmental decisions relating to the proposed relocation of a utility cabinet within approximately
475 feet of your residence?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions because they would
not have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your residence.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City of Pacific Grove will soon consider whether to enter into a contract with Monterey
One Water, a regional agency that provides wastewater treatment and recycling services to the City
and other local government agencies, to relocate a utility cabinet used to operate a sewage lift
station on the seaward side of Oceanside Boulevard. The utility cabinet and lift station are
components of the City's sewer collection system, and are owned by Monterey One Water. The

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-023
Page No. 2

utility cabinet would be relocated to City-owned property at the northern end of Esplanade Park,
across Oceanview Boulevard from the lift station.

Your residence, a single-family home within a neighborhood of single-family homes, is
located within 475 feet of the proposed location for the relocation of the utility cabinet. You own
your residence, and it is valued at more than $2,000. Although the precise design of the utility
cabinet has not been finalized, you state that the height, length, or width of the utility cabinet will
not exceed several feet. Due to intervening homes, trees, and topography, the relocated utility
cabinet would not be visible from your residence.

ANALYSIS

Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Act prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on an
official's financial interest. Section 87103 identifies the interests that may give rise to a
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act. You have a real-property interest in your residence
under Section 87103(b) because you have a direct interest in it worth $2,000 or more.

Foreseeability & Materiality

For a financial interest that is not a named party in, or the subject of, the decision at issue,
the financial effect of that decision on an official's financial interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
"can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation
18701(b).) Your residence is not a named party in, or the subject of, the decisions relating to the
proposed relocation of the utility cabinet.

Under the Act's 500-foot rule, set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a)(11), a decision's effect on
an official's real-property interest is material unless there are sufficient facts to indicate that there
will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property. The relocated utility
cabinet will not be visible from your residence due to intervening homes, trees, and topography.
The facts presented provide no indication that the decisions at issue would have a measurable
impact on your residence. Thus, the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

[signature]

By:    Matthew F. Christy
       Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl
